era_context: The Roman Empire under Tiberius (14–37 CE) in its final years, transitioning toward Caligula's reign (37–41 CE). Early Christian movement emerging in Judea and spreading to diaspora communities. Han Dynasty in China (206 BCE–220 CE) in its later phase. Parthian Empire dominant in Mesopotamia and Persia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia trade routes (limited numismatic/archaeological evidence for this specific year)

events.title: Death of Emperor Tiberius
events.description: Tiberius, Roman Emperor since 14 CE, died on 16 March 37 CE (not 35 CE; this entry corrects a common dating error). However, if the year 35 is intended, it falls during Tiberius's final years of rule, marked by increasing paranoia, purges of the Senate, and withdrawal to Capri. His reign saw consolidation of imperial power and military stability on most frontiers, though his later years were characterized by political instability and the rise of Sejanus's faction (until his fall in 31 CE). The succession crisis that would lead to Caligula's accession was already forming.
key_figures: Caligula/Gaius (future Emperor, then in his mid-20s)
events.certainty_note: Tiberius died in 37 CE, not 35 CE. If 35 CE is the intended research year, this event is misdated in the prompt. The year 35 CE falls during Tiberius's reign but no specific major event is definitively dated to this year in surviving sources.

events.title: Conversion of Saul of Tarsus (traditional dating)
events.description: Christian tradition places the conversion of Saul (later Paul the Apostle) at approximately 33–35 CE, though the exact date is highly uncertain. According to Acts 9, Saul experienced a vision of the risen Jesus while traveling to Damascus to persecute Christians. This event is foundational to Christian history, as Paul became the primary missionary to gentiles and author of much of the New Testament. However, Paul's own letters (Galatians 1:15–17) provide minimal chronological detail, and the Acts account was written decades later.
key_figures: Saul of Tarsus / Paul the Apostle
events.certainty_note: The conversion is attested in early Christian sources but the precise year is not given in any contemporary document. Scholars estimate 33–36 CE based on Paul's own chronological hints and the timeline of Acts, but this remains speculative. The date 35 CE is a conventional scholarly estimate, not a confirmed historical fact.

events.title: Han Dynasty administrative reforms under Emperor Chengdi
events.description: Emperor Chengdi of Han (r. 33–7 BCE) ruled during a period of internal instability and external pressure from the Xiongnu. The year 35 CE falls after Chengdi's reign (he died in 7 BCE), so this entry requires correction. If the intended year is 35 BCE, Chengdi was in the midst of his reign, dealing with court factionalism, the influence of the Consort families (particularly the Wang family), and military challenges on the northern frontier. His reign saw significant shifts in power toward the imperial consorts' families, which would culminate in the Wang family's usurpation in 9 CE.
key_figures: Wang Mang (future usurper, then rising in influence)

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: If the year is 35 CE, it falls well after Chengdi's death (7 BCE). If 35 BCE is intended, Chengdi was ruling but no specific event is definitively dated to this year. The broader context of consort family influence is well-attested.
cross_references: 9-001 (Wang Mang's usurpation and founding of Xin Dynasty)

disconfirming_evidence: The primary issue with year 35 CE is that no major historical events are definitively dated to this specific year in surviving sources. Tiberius's death is often conflated with 35 CE in popular accounts, but he actually died in 37 CE. The conversion of Paul is traditionally dated to 33–35 CE, but this is a scholarly estimate with no contemporary confirmation. The year 35 CE may be historically significant primarily as a point within larger processes (Tiberius's reign, early Christian expansion, Han Dynasty decline) rather than as a year marked by discrete, datable events.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 35 CE is sparse. Roman sources (Tacitus, Suetonius, Cassius Dio) were written 80–200 years after the events they describe and often focus on dramatic moments (deaths, scandals) rather than routine administrative years. Christian sources (Acts, Paul's letters) are theologically motivated and provide minimal chronological precision. Chinese sources (Han Shu) are more systematic but were compiled centuries after the events. The year 35 CE falls in a period of relative stability in the Roman Empire and Han China, which may explain why no specific events are prominently recorded. Western-centric historiography may also obscure significant events in other regions (Parthia, India, Southeast Asia) for which sources are sparse.

graph_edges.note: Tiberius's reign (and the political tensions of 35 CE) led directly to his death and Caligula's accession in 37 CE
Paul's conversion initiated his missionary career, leading to his travels and epistles in the 40s–60s CE
